planting.note: Set dormant bare-root trees in early spring or in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Plant this vigorous triploid with the graft union clear of the soil and give it plenty of room, as it makes a large spreading tree. It needs two other compatible pollinators nearby since its own pollen is sterile. Pick the large tart green cooking fruit in mid-autumn; the flesh breaks down to a fluffy puree when baked.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in late winter to early spring.

directions: This reliable early cooker crops heavily and resists scab, so it suits damp gardens. Keep the young tree well watered through its first summers and thin crowded fruitlets in early summer. Harvest the pale green apples from late summer; they cook down quickly to a sharp sauce.

planting.note: Establish dormant trees in early spring in deep fertile soil.

directions: A large old dual-purpose variety that both bakes well and sweetens in storage for eating. Give it a sunny open site and prune out crossing wood each winter to keep the crown airy. Pick the red-flushed fruit in mid-autumn and let it mellow for a few weeks before using.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; tolerant of heavier clay soils.

directions: This hardy Victorian cooker copes with wet clay better than most and blossoms late enough to dodge spring frosts. Mulch the root zone each spring and thin heavy clusters for larger fruit. Gather the angular green apples in autumn; they turn to a firm pale puree that needs little sugar.

planting.note: Set dormant trees in early spring; a naturally compact grower.

directions: A compact early cooker that suits smaller gardens and bears large pale fruit while still young. Thin fruitlets so the branches are not overloaded and support them if a heavy crop sets. Pick from late summer and use promptly, as the soft flesh does not keep long.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in fertile ground.

directions: Prized by cooks for a fine sharp flavor, this old golden-skinned apple cooks to a smooth translucent puree. Give it a sunny sheltered spot and prune lightly to keep an open goblet shape. Harvest the round yellow fruit in early autumn and store the crop cool for a few weeks of keeping.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; happiest in a warm sheltered site.

directions: Famous for very large handsome dual-purpose fruit that both bakes whole and eats fresh. Thin the crop hard so each remaining apple can size up fully and protect the blossom from late frost. Pick the big red-striped apples in early autumn and handle them gently, as they bruise easily.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a vigorous triploid.

directions: A big vigorous triploid cooker bearing very large pale green fruit that cooks to a sharp froth. It needs two nearby pollinators because it cannot fertilize others. Give it space and rich soil, and harvest the heavy crop in mid-autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; crops young and reliably.

directions: One of the earliest cooking apples, ready in high summer when other fruit is still green. It is a heavy cropper that benefits from firm thinning to prevent biennial bearing. Pick the pale green apples from midsummer and cook them straight away, as they do not store.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm sheltered spot.

directions: A famous old russet with an intense sharp, pear-like flavor that deepens in storage. It ripens late and needs a warm site to develop its best taste. Pick the dull green-brown fruit in mid-autumn and keep it cool for several weeks before eating.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; needs a warm, well-drained site.

directions: The classic aromatic English dessert apple, complex and richly flavored but demanding of good conditions. Grow it in a sheltered, sunny spot and watch for scab and canker in damp climates. Harvest the orange-flushed fruit in early autumn once it parts easily from the spur.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; tolerant and easy to grow.

directions: A reliable, easy russet with dense nutty flesh and a distinctive dry skin. It is compact and upright, tolerating cooler sites where richer dessert apples struggle. Pick the golden-brown fruit in early autumn and enjoy it fresh over the following weeks.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a large vigorous triploid.

directions: A grand old dual-purpose triploid with a nutty flavor, good both fresh and baked. It grows into a big tree and needs two other pollinators as its own pollen is infertile. Harvest the large orange-red apples in mid-autumn; they mellow further in store.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a vigorous triploid variety.

directions: A rich, aromatic old Yorkshire apple and reputed parent of Cox's Orange Pippin. As a triploid it sets a light crop and needs two compatible pollinators nearby. Pick the yellow-and-red fruit in early autumn and eat it within a couple of months for the fullest flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm, sunny position.

directions: A richly flavored old reinette often called one of the finest dessert apples. It ripens late and needs warmth and sun to reach its full nutty, aromatic character. Harvest the golden russeted fruit in mid-autumn and store it cool to eat through early winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; blossoms very late.

directions: A very old flat-shaped apple that blooms so late it usually escapes spring frosts, making it valuable in cold gardens. The dense fruit has a rich, slightly acid flavor and keeps well. Pick in mid-autumn and store the crop cool to eat into winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; prefers a warm, sheltered site.

directions: A classic French dessert and culinary apple, ribbed and pale, famous in tarts and noted for high vitamin C. It needs a long warm season to ripen its complex spicy flavor. Harvest the lumpy yellow-green fruit in mid-autumn and let it mellow in storage.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; tolerates drier soils well.

directions: An old Essex apple with a distinctive nutmeg-spice flavor that develops in storage. It thrives on lighter, drier soils and keeps exceptionally well into late winter. Pick the greenish russeted fruit late in autumn and store it for two months before eating.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; bears small, richly flavored fruit.

directions: A small golden russet with a distinctive honeyed, faintly pineapple-like flavor. The fruit is little but borne in abundance, so heavy thinning is rarely needed. Pick the yellow-brown apples in early autumn and enjoy them fresh soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an exceptional keeper.

directions: Reputed to be the oldest named American apple, a sturdy russet good for eating, cider and long keeping. Give it a sunny open site and it will crop dependably for generations. Pick the greenish-brown fruit in mid-autumn and store it cool, where it lasts into spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; prized for cider and keeping.

directions: An old American russet with dense sugary flesh, valued both for fresh eating and for making a rich hard cider. It stores remarkably well and its high sugar makes strong juice. Harvest the bronze-gold fruit in mid-autumn and keep it cool for months of use.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; give it good soil and care.

directions: A celebrated old New York dessert apple, richly spicy and reputedly a favorite of Thomas Jefferson. It can be fussy and prone to scab, so plant it in a well-drained, airy site. Harvest the deep red fruit in mid-autumn and store it cool to develop full flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a long-keeping classic.

directions: A historic green American apple used fresh, for cooking and for fine cider, famed for keeping through winter. It needs a long season to ripen its bright acid-sweet flavor. Pick the firm green fruit in mid-autumn and store it cool, where the flavor improves for months.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a vigorous triploid cooker.

directions: A classic tart green American cooking apple, long favored for pies because it holds its shape when baked. As a triploid it needs two other pollinators nearby to set fruit. Harvest the grass-green apples in mid-autumn and use or store them cool through early winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a robust old New England apple.

directions: A hardy, vigorous nineteenth-century apple good for eating, cooking and cider, once the mainstay of New England orchards. It tends toward biennial bearing, so thin the crop in heavy years. Pick the red-blushed fruit in mid-autumn and store it cool, where it keeps for months.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; slow to begin bearing.

directions: A famous late American apple with crisp, sprightly flesh valued for pies, cider and fresh eating. It is slow to come into bearing but then crops heavily and keeps late blossom that dodges frost. Harvest the red-striped fruit in mid to late autumn and store it cool into winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an early triploid variety.

directions: A very old, aromatic early apple excellent for sauce, baking and fresh eating. Being a triploid it produces sterile pollen and needs two other pollinators nearby. Pick the red-streaked fruit from late summer and use it promptly, as it does not keep long.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; very cold hardy.

directions: An extremely cold-hardy Minnesota apple bred for harsh winters, good for cooking and fresh eating. It bears young and heavily, so thin the fruit to keep annual crops. Harvest the red-flushed apples in early autumn and use them within a couple of months.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; hardy and disease tolerant.

directions: A very hardy old cooking apple famous for its enormous fruit, a single apple sometimes filling a pie. It resists disease well and tolerates cold, difficult sites. Pick the huge red-striped apples in early autumn and use them for baking and sauce.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a very hardy Russian apple.

directions: An old Russian apple of exceptional cold hardiness, tart and best used for cooking and sauce. It blossoms and crops reliably even in short-season northern gardens. Harvest the red-striped fruit in late summer and use it soon, as it ripens early and keeps poorly.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an old New England dessert apple.

directions: A colonial New England apple with tender, nutty flesh once thought so good you need seek no further. Give it a sunny open site and prune to keep the crown light. Pick the dull red russeted fruit in mid-autumn and eat it fresh over the following weeks.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a rich late dessert apple.

directions: An old West Country apple with an intense, aromatic clove-scented flavor and knobbly ribbed fruit. It crops lightly and grows rather awkwardly, so train it with care. Pick the dark red-brown apples in mid-autumn and store them to eat through winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; suits cooler, wetter regions.

directions: An old dark-red West Country dessert apple with a crisp, wine-like flavor, tolerant of damp western climates. It bears early in the season and blossoms freely. Pick the deep crimson fruit from late summer and eat it soon, as it does not keep long.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a reliable early dessert apple.

directions: A popular old English early apple with a sweet strawberry-scented flavor when fully ripe. It is easy and reliable, a good pollinator and a heavy cropper. Pick the bright red fruit from late summer and eat it straight from the tree, as it soon goes soft.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an early russet.

directions: An early golden russet with soft, juicy, pear-like flesh and a rich flavor. It is one of the best-tasting early apples but must be eaten soon after picking. Harvest the golden-brown fruit in early autumn and enjoy it fresh within a couple of weeks.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; needs a long, warm season.

directions: A very late-keeping green apple that ripens its sharp, rich flavor only with a long warm autumn. It hangs late on the tree and stores into spring. Pick the firm green-yellow fruit as late as possible in autumn and keep it cool for months of use.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; classic cider and keeping apple.

directions: An old American apple with a spicy, wine-like flavor, long used for cider, cooking and fresh eating. Its pollen is poor, so plant it with two other pollinators. Harvest the deep red fruit in mid to late autumn and store it cool, where it keeps well into winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a seedling of Winesap.

directions: A rich, tart-sweet American apple raised from Winesap, good for eating, cooking and cider. The fruit can crack in wet seasons, so even watering helps. Pick the dull red apples in mid-autumn and store them cool for winter use.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an outstanding keeper.

directions: A striking dark-red, almost black southern apple, very firm and best after months in storage. The hard flesh softens and sweetens over winter and it makes good cider. Harvest the deep purple-red fruit in mid to late autumn and store it cool for months before eating.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a very hardy Maine apple.

directions: An old Maine apple with dark purplish skin, good for pies, cider and remarkable long keeping. Its flavor improves through winter storage and it resists disease well. Pick the deep-colored fruit in mid-autumn and keep it cool, where it lasts until spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: A celebrated bittersharp vintage cider apple that can make a balanced single-variety cider on its own. It can be a shy, uneven cropper and prefers a warm, well-drained site. Harvest the dark red fruit in mid-autumn and mill it soon for pressing.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a compact, reliable cropper.

directions: A classic bittersweet cider apple that ferments to a soft, full-bodied juice, often used on its own. It is compact, spur-bearing and dependable, cropping regularly. Pick the small red-flushed fruit in mid to late autumn and let it mellow briefly before milling.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a vigorous cider variety.

directions: A vigorous bittersweet cider apple giving a soft, aromatic juice with good body. It crops heavily and regularly, making it a mainstay of West Country orchards. Harvest the red-striped fruit in mid to late autumn and press it once it has softened.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; heavy, regular cropper.

directions: A French-derived bittersweet cider apple valued for very heavy, reliable crops and easy management. Its juice is medium bittersweet and blends well with sharper varieties. Pick the small yellow-green fruit in mid-autumn and mill it promptly for the press.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a strongly bittersweet variety.

directions: A strongly bittersweet cider apple with high tannin, used to give body and grip to blends. It can bear biennially, so thinning in heavy years helps even the crop. Harvest the dark red fruit in mid-autumn and blend the sharp tannic juice with milder varieties.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a late bittersweet cider apple.

directions: A late-ripening bittersweet cider apple prized for very high tannin and full-bodied juice. It ferments to a rich, astringent cider that adds structure to blends. Pick the small russeted fruit late in autumn and press it after a short mellowing.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an ancient bittersharp variety.

directions: A very old Herefordshire bittersharp cider apple giving an intensely aromatic, acidic juice. It contributes sharpness and a distinctive fruity aroma to cider blends. Harvest the red-striped fruit in mid-autumn and mill it soon for pressing.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a late bittersweet cropper.

directions: A late bittersweet cider apple named for the russet patch at its base, giving a mild, soft juice. It crops regularly and blends well with sharper cider varieties. Pick the small russet-nosed fruit late in autumn and press it after a short store.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a mid-season bittersweet.

directions: A Somerset bittersweet cider apple giving a full-bodied, softly tannic juice. It is a good regular cropper and a reliable orchard tree. Harvest the small red-flushed fruit in mid to late autumn and mill it once softened.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an early bittersweet cropper.

directions: An early-ripening bittersweet cider apple giving a mild, sweet juice with soft tannin. It bears heavily and regularly, a dependable choice for the cider orchard. Pick the red-streaked fruit in early to mid-autumn and press it promptly.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an early bittersweet variety.

directions: An early bittersweet cider apple giving a soft, aromatic, medium-tannin juice. It crops early in the cider season, extending the pressing window. Harvest the red-flushed fruit in early autumn and mill it soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a late bittersharp variety.

directions: A late bittersharp cider apple from Somerset giving a rich, sharp, tannic juice. It tends to bear biennially, so thin heavy crops to even out the years. Pick the small dark red fruit late in autumn and press it after a short mellowing.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a late bittersharp cider apple.

directions: A late bittersharp cider apple giving a well-balanced, aromatic juice good enough for single-variety cider. The tree is fairly compact and crops reliably. Harvest the dark red fruit late in autumn and mill it once it has softened slightly.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a sweet cider variety.

directions: A sweet cider apple giving a mild, low-tannin juice used to soften sharper blends. It is a heavy, regular cropper and an easy orchard tree. Pick the pale green-yellow fruit in mid to late autumn and press it soon after.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a full sweet cider apple.

directions: A traditional sweet cider apple giving a mild, aromatic juice low in acid and tannin. It blends well to add sweetness and body to a cider. Harvest the yellow-green fruit in mid-autumn and mill it promptly for pressing.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an early bittersweet variety.

directions: An early bittersweet cider apple giving a soft, full-bodied juice with gentle tannin. It ripens early, opening the cider-pressing season. Pick the red-flushed fruit in early autumn and press it soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; an early bittersweet variety.

directions: An early bittersweet cider apple bred at Long Ashton, giving a juice with good tannin and body. It crops early and heavily, useful for starting the pressing season. Harvest the red-colored fruit in early autumn and mill it promptly.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a vigorous French-type cider apple.

directions: A vigorous French-derived bittersweet cider apple valued for early, heavy crops and disease resistance. Its juice is soft and full, a good base for blending. Pick the yellow-green fruit in early to mid-autumn and press it soon after.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a late bittersweet cider apple.

directions: A late-ripening Norman bittersweet cider apple giving a soft, full-bodied juice for blending. It crops well and hangs late on the tree. Harvest the small russeted fruit late in autumn and mill it after a brief mellowing.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a French bittersweet cider apple.

directions: A French bittersweet cider apple giving a richly tannic, aromatic juice. It contributes strong body and color to traditional cider blends. Pick the small dark red fruit in mid to late autumn and press it once it softens.

common_name: Reine des Reinettes (King of the Pippins) Apple

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a dual-purpose old variety.

directions: An old golden dual-purpose apple, aromatic for eating and firm enough for cooking. It is reliable and a good pollinator, cropping regularly. Harvest the yellow-flushed fruit in early autumn and store it cool for several weeks of use.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Site this vigorous tree in full sun and stake it during the first season while the framework establishes. Prune to an open center each winter and provide a compatible pollinizer such as Bartlett or Comice. Harvest the long-necked russeted fruit firm and mature; Bosc holds its shape well and stores longer than most European pears.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring before growth begins.

directions: Give this productive tree fertile, well-drained ground and a nearby pollen partner. Thin the crop after fruit set so the remaining pears size up evenly. The egg-shaped green fruit stays firm on the tree, so pick it mature and chill it briefly before ripening at room temperature.

planting.note: Set out dormant trees in early spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Choose a warm, wind-protected site because this dessert pear crops best with steady summer heat. Plant with a reliable pollinizer and keep the soil evenly moist during fruit swell. Harvest the plump, sweet fruit firm and let it soften indoors until the buttery flesh is ready to eat.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring; partially self-fertile.

directions: This reliable European variety sets some fruit on its own but crops far heavier with a partner nearby. Prune lightly each winter to keep an open, productive canopy. Pick the long, slightly russeted fruit firm and let it ripen off the tree to a sweet, juicy finish.

planting.note: Set out dormant trees in early spring; compact and easy to manage.

directions: A Conference and Comice cross, this variety grows compactly and comes into bearing young. Give it full sun and a compatible pollinizer for dependable crops. Harvest the elongated, vanilla-sweet fruit firm; it ripens smoothly and resists browning when cut.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: This small heirloom pear develops a striking red freckle over yellow skin as it ripens. Plant with another European variety for pollination and thin the crop for better size. Harvest the fruit firm in autumn and store cold; the trout-like blush deepens as it reaches eating ripeness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; very cold hardy.

directions: This early-ripening, cold-hardy variety resembles Bartlett and suits northern gardens. Provide a compatible pollinizer and watch for fire blight in wet springs. Pick the fruit firm before it fully colors, since it softens quickly and does not hold long once ripe.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; exceptionally cold hardy.

directions: An old Belgian variety, this vigorous tree withstands hard winters and bears aromatic fruit. Plant with another European pear and protect against pear scab in humid regions. Harvest the rounded, blushed fruit firm and ripen it indoors to a rich, sweet flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; very adaptable and heat tolerant.

directions: This tough hybrid resists fire blight and tolerates heat and humidity where dessert pears struggle. It is partially self-fertile but crops better with a partner, and it bears young and heavily. Harvest the gritty, firm fruit in autumn for cooking, canning, and preserves.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; strong blight resistance.

directions: Bred for fire blight resistance, this upright tree suits humid climates and bears early. Plant with another variety such as Bartlett for cross-pollination. Harvest the soft, mild fruit firm and ripen indoors for fresh eating; it makes an excellent pollinizer for other pears.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; good disease resistance.

directions: This blight-resistant variety yields sweet, aromatic fruit with soft, juicy flesh. Its pollen is sterile, so plant it near two other pears that can pollinate each other and it. Harvest the medium fruit firm in autumn and finish ripening it off the tree.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; excellent blight resistance.

directions: A Canadian introduction, this late-season variety combines strong fire blight resistance with fine dessert quality. Provide a compatible pollinizer and prune to keep the canopy open. Harvest the smooth, sweet fruit firm in autumn; it stores well for several weeks under cool conditions.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; highly blight resistant.

directions: This early, blight-resistant variety produces smooth-textured fruit resembling Bartlett. Plant it with another European pear for pollination and site it in full sun. Harvest the fruit firm before it softens on the tree, then ripen indoors for best eating quality.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; vigorous and frost tolerant at bloom.

directions: This vigorous French variety tolerates cooler sites and its blossoms resist late frost fairly well. Give it room to grow and a compatible pollen partner. Harvest the russeted, rose-scented fruit firm and ripen it indoors to a soft, buttery texture.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; reliable and heavy cropping.

directions: This old, dependable variety crops heavily and colors with an attractive red flush on the sunny side. Plant with a compatible pollinizer, avoiding partners in the same incompatibility group. Harvest the fruit firm in autumn and ripen it indoors to a juicy, slightly tangy sweetness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; late-ripening and long-keeping.

directions: This late-season variety stores exceptionally well and holds its quality through winter. The tree is somewhat spreading, so prune to maintain a workable shape, and provide a pollinizer. Harvest the small russeted fruit firm in late autumn and keep it cold before ripening as needed.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: This Australian variety resembles Bartlett but ripens later and stores longer. Plant with a compatible European pollinizer and keep the soil evenly moist during fruit development. Harvest the knobbly green fruit firm and ripen it indoors to a smooth, sweet, juicy finish.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: This Italian variety produces a long, tapered fruit prized for its sweet, aromatic flesh. It performs best in warm regions and needs a compatible pollen partner. Harvest the elongated fruit firm in autumn and ripen it indoors until the neck softens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; partially self-fertile.

directions: This Portuguese variety is hardy, productive, and sets some fruit without a partner. It develops a characteristic russet patch around the eye of each fruit. Harvest firm in autumn and ripen indoors; the fruit stores well and keeps a pleasant sweet flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; compact and early cropping.

directions: This compact British variety bears early and reliably, making it a good choice for smaller gardens. Provide a compatible pollinizer and thin the sometimes heavy crop. Harvest the small, juicy fruit firm in late summer and ripen it quickly indoors, as it does not keep long.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: A Comice-derived variety, this pear offers rich dessert quality with juicy, melting flesh. Note that it is incompatible with Doyenné du Comice, so choose a different pollinizer. Harvest the fruit firm in early autumn and eat it soon, as it ripens fast and keeps only briefly.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; large fruited and vigorous.

directions: This old French variety produces very large, knobbly fruit and often crops best when grown on a warm wall. Provide a compatible pollen partner and rich, moist soil. Harvest the big fruit firm in autumn and ripen it indoors to a juicy, sweet finish.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: This late-ripening Belgian variety needs a warm, sheltered position to mature its rich, sweet fruit. Plant with a compatible pollinizer and protect it from cold winds. Harvest firm in late autumn and store cool; the fruit ripens to a smooth, buttery texture in winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a red-skinned Bartlett sport.

directions: This red-skinned sport of Bartlett shares its parent's classic flavor with brighter color. Plant with another European variety and thin the crop for good fruit size. Harvest the bell-shaped fruit firm and green-tinged, then ripen it indoors until it colors and softens at the neck.

planting.note: Named cultivars are grafted; seedlings need cold stratification and take years to fruit.

directions: The wild species forms a long-lived tree and serves mainly as rootstock and breeding parent. Seeds require several weeks of cold, moist stratification before they will sprout, and seedlings vary widely and fruit slowly. Grow named grafted cultivars for predictable fruit and cross-pollinate with another European variety.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a popular russet Asian pear.

directions: This favorite russet-brown Asian pear delivers sweet, juicy, low-acid fruit with a crisp bite. Plant near another Asian variety such as Shinseiki or Chojuro for pollination and thin heavily. Harvest the fruit tree-ripe when it colors fully, since Asian pears reach peak flavor before picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; partially self-fertile.

directions: This yellow-skinned Asian pear is partly self-fertile and easy to grow, though it fruits better with a partner. It stores well and keeps its crisp texture for weeks. Harvest the round fruit when it turns fully yellow and eat it firm and crunchy straight from the tree.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; russeted, long-keeping fruit.

directions: This russet Asian pear offers a distinctive rich, butterscotch-like flavor and stores exceptionally well. Plant with another Asian variety for cross-pollination and thin the crop for size. Harvest the flat-round fruit when the brown skin colors evenly and it snaps crisply.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; early-ripening dessert pear.

directions: This early, sweet Asian pear is highly regarded in Japan for its juicy, fine-textured flesh. Plant with a compatible Asian pollinizer and thin fruit to improve size. Harvest the small russet fruit tree-ripe when it colors fully for the best flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; classic green-skinned nashi.

directions: Also sold as 20th Century, this famous Japanese pear has smooth yellow-green skin and clean, crisp, juicy flesh. Plant with another Asian variety and protect the thin skin from scab and insects. Harvest the round fruit tree-ripe when it turns pale yellow-green and eat it firm.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; good disease resistance.

directions: This late-season russet Asian pear resists fire blight better than many and stores well. Plant with a compatible Asian pollinizer and thin the crop for larger fruit. Harvest the round, golden-brown fruit tree-ripe when it colors fully, then keep it cool for extended storage.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; large fruited late variety.

directions: This vigorous variety produces very large, russeted fruit popular in Korean and Japanese growing. Its pollen is poor, so plant it near two other Asian pears that pollinate each other. Harvest the big fruit tree-ripe when the brown skin colors evenly and it eats crisp and sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; very large, long-storing fruit.

directions: Also called Dan Bae, this variety bears huge, crisp, sweet russet pears that store for months. Plant with a compatible Asian pollinizer and thin hard to manage the heavy crop. Harvest the fruit late and tree-ripe when the brown skin colors fully for maximum sweetness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; richly flavored russet pear.

directions: This russet Asian pear is prized for its rich, aromatic, butterscotch-toned flavor. Plant with another Asian variety for pollination and thin fruit for good size. Harvest tree-ripe when the brown skin colors evenly and the fruit is crisp and juicy.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; early-blooming Chinese white pear.

directions: This Chinese white pear bears pear-shaped fruit with tender, sweet, aromatic flesh. It blooms early, so site it where late frost is less likely and plant a compatible pollinizer such as Tsu Li. Harvest tree-ripe when the greenish-yellow skin lightens and the fruit eats crisp.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; long-storing Chinese pear.

directions: This vigorous Chinese pear produces firm, egg-shaped fruit that stores for many months. It is a good pollen partner for Ya Li, and the two are often planted together. Harvest the fruit firm in autumn and let it mellow in cool storage before eating.

planting.note: Extremely cold-hardy species used for breeding and rootstock; seeds need stratification.

directions: This exceptionally cold-hardy species survives harsh northern winters and parents many hardy pear cultivars. Seeds need several weeks of cold, moist stratification before sowing, and seedlings are variable. Grow it for breeding, rootstock, and cold-climate fruit, cross-pollinating with another pear.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; bred for extreme cold hardiness.

directions: Developed in Minnesota, this very hardy variety thrives where most European pears freeze out. It eats crisp and mild rather than soft and buttery, and it resists fire blight well. Harvest the fruit firm while the skin still shows a green blush, before it becomes grainy.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; cold hardy and disease tolerant.

directions: This hardy South Dakota introduction yields small, very sweet, juicy fruit and resists fire blight. Its pollen is poor, so plant it near another pear for reliable cross-pollination. Harvest the fruit firm in early autumn and finish ripening it indoors for a rich, sweet flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; heat and blight tolerant hybrid.

directions: This blight-resistant hybrid handles Southern heat and humidity and bears large, firm fruit. It is a dependable pollinizer and crops heavily, making it good for canning and preserves. Harvest the round fruit firm in late summer and cook or can it, as the flesh is somewhat gritty.

planting.note: Set out dormant bare-root or potted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained loam with the graft union above the soil line, and stake young trees against wind. Prune lightly in winter to keep an open goblet shape, since quince fruits on the tips of the previous year's growth. Leave the hard, fragrant fruit on the tree until deep gold in mid to late autumn, then cook or store it to soften.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees while dormant in early spring.

directions: Give this Russian selection full sun and rich, moisture-retentive soil, planting the graft union a few inches proud of the ground. Its pineapple-scented fruit is tender enough to eat fresh off the tree when fully ripe, unlike most cooking quinces. Harvest when the skin turns clear yellow and the fruit gives off a strong perfume in early autumn.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: This old apple-shaped quince wants full sun and steady moisture in deep, fertile soil. Keep the center open with light winter pruning and mulch to hold water through summer. The round fruit ripens to a rich orange-yellow in early autumn and softens with cooking into a fragrant, rose-tinted preserve.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in full sun.

directions: A vigorous Turkish variety that appreciates a long, warm season to ripen its very large fruit. Plant in full sun with fertile soil and thin the crop so branches are not overloaded. Harvest the big, downy quinces when they turn bright yellow in autumn, then store or cook them once the fuzz rubs away.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: A reliable, productive quince that bears young and tolerates a range of soils as long as drainage is good. Plant in full sun and keep watered while fruit is swelling in summer. The pear-shaped, greenish-yellow fruit ripens late and has a delicate flavor that comes forward after cooking.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: Another Burbank introduction, this quince is early-bearing and productive in a sunny site with deep, fertile soil. Water through dry spells and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool. Pick the large, spicy-flavored fruit in early to mid autumn as it colors up gold.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this small, picturesquely gnarled tree in full sun to light shade in any decent, well-drained soil. It needs little pruning beyond removing crossing branches in winter. Pick the brown, open-eyed fruit after the first frosts in late autumn, then let it soften and 'blet' indoors for a few weeks until the flesh turns sweet and spiced.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: A dependable, ornamental medlar with a spreading crown and good autumn color, easy in most well-drained soils. Give it full sun for best fruiting and prune only to shape while dormant. Harvest the medium fruit after leaf fall and let it soften indoors until the flesh becomes brown and custard-like.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: Grown for its unusually large fruit, this Dutch medlar makes a sturdy small tree in full sun and free-draining soil. Little pruning is needed once a strong framework is set. Pick the big brown fruit late in the season and blet it soft for several weeks before enjoying its sweet, aromatic pulp.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: A hardy, vigorous medlar that carries large fruit and stands up to cold winters better than most. Plant in full sun with good drainage and allow room for its broad, low crown. Harvest after the first frosts and store the fruit until it bletts to a soft, spiced brown.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: A productive Scandinavian selection grown commercially for its large, heavy trusses of dark berries. Site in full sun for the best crop and fall color, and water through dry spells while fruit is filling. Harvest the near-black fruit in late summer once it softens slightly, then juice or freeze it since it is astringent fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in most soils.

directions: A compact, hardy cultivar bred in central Europe for reliable, abundant fruiting on a manageable bush. Give it full sun and even moisture, and remove the oldest stems every few years to renew the shrub. Pick the large black berries in late summer for juice, syrup, or drying.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: A tidy selection valued for brilliant red-purple fall foliage as well as its fruit. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil and mulch the roots. Enjoy the white spring bloom, then gather the black berries in late summer for cooking while the leaves color up in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; very cold hardy.

directions: A tall, vigorous conservation selection chosen for hardiness, windbreak use, and wildlife value on the northern plains. Plant in full sun in almost any soil and let it sucker into a thicket if desired. It produces plentiful dark fruit in late summer that birds relish and that people can juice or preserve.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: An improved red chokeberry selected for glossier leaves, superior scarlet fall color, and larger, more abundant berries. Grow in full sun in average to moist soil and prune out old canes to keep it dense. The vivid red fruit forms in autumn and clings through much of winter, feeding birds and available for jelly.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: An extremely hardy, suckering shrub of the northern prairies grown for its sweet, blueberry-like fruit. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and thin out crowded stems to keep the bush productive. White spring flowers ripen into purple berries in early summer that are excellent fresh, dried, or baked into pies.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: A naturally dwarf saskatoon that stays low and rounded, making it easy to net and pick. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil and water during fruit set for the sweetest berries. Harvest the deep purple fruit in early to midsummer when it comes away easily from the cluster.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: A classic commercial saskatoon prized for heavy yields of large, sweet, mild berries with little astringency. Plant in full sun and give the suckering clump room to form a productive hedge. Pick the dusty-blue fruit in midsummer once it is fully colored and softens to the touch.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: A heavy-bearing, strongly suckering saskatoon that fruits evenly and is easy to establish as a row or hedge. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and renew old canes periodically. Its large, sweet berries ripen uniformly in midsummer, which makes for efficient hand or mechanical picking.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: One of the largest-fruited saskatoons, tall and early-ripening but tending to bear its berries over a longer window. Plant in full sun with good drainage and expect a vigorous, upright bush. Pick the big, sweet, juicy fruit in early summer, returning over a couple of weeks as it ripens in stages.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: A productive Saskatchewan selection bearing large berries in long clusters and showing good disease resistance. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil and thin the suckers to maintain vigor. The sweet fruit ripens over an extended midsummer period, rewarding several picking passes.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to part shade.

directions: A popular small multi-stemmed tree grown for clouds of white spring bloom, fiery orange-red fall color, and edible fruit. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil and water it through its first summers. Its purple-black berries sweeten in early summer, though birds often reach them first.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist soil.

directions: An upright, suckering native large shrub or small tree of moist woodland edges and swamp margins. Plant in sun or part shade in soil that stays reasonably moist, allowing room for its clumping habit. White flowers open very early, and the dark red-purple fruit ripens in early summer, sweet and useful for pies where the birds leave any behind.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: A Mediterranean hawthorn grown for centuries for its comparatively large, flavorful fruit, well suited to warm, dry climates. Plant in full sun in free-draining soil and water young trees until established, after which it is quite drought tolerant. The apple-scented yellow to orange-red fruit ripens in autumn and is eaten fresh or made into preserves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Grown across China for its large, tart red haws used in the candied 'tanghulu' skewers and haw flakes. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and prune to a manageable open crown. The glossy red fruit, larger than most hawthorns, ripens in autumn and is cooked, dried, or made into sweets and drinks.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the tree in a sunny bed and water deeply through the first summer to establish the roots. Its showy pink blossoms are frost-sensitive, so choose a rise rather than a low pocket where cold air settles. The large white-fleshed freestone with a crimson-blushed skin ripens in late summer and softens quickly once picked.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; one of the best choices for cold-winter gardens.

directions: Choose a sheltered, sunny site and plant while the tree is dormant, keeping the graft above the soil. This variety is prized for bud hardiness and often sets a crop after winters that damage other peaches. The medium yellow freestones with a dull-red blush ripen in midsummer and are excellent fresh or frozen.

planting.note: Set out dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot with the union above grade and mulch the root zone to conserve moisture. Thin the fruit in spring so the remaining peaches size up and the branches do not overload. This late-season yellow freestone resists browning, has little fuzz, and ripens firm enough to handle well.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set the tree at the same depth it grew in the nursery, keeping the graft clear of soil. Prune to an open center each winter to let light reach the fruiting wood. The large, nearly fuzz-free yellow freestone develops a bright red blush and ripens in late summer with rich, sweet flavor.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: Plant in full sun and deep, well-drained soil, watering steadily through the first season. This vigorous variety needs firm dormant pruning to keep fruiting wood low and productive. The large, firm yellow freestone with a heavy red blush ripens in late summer and stores and ships unusually well.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Set out in full sun with the graft above the soil and stake for the first year. Thin the crop in late spring to keep fruit large and branches from breaking. The hardy, cold-tolerant tree yields juicy yellow freestones with a red blush that ripen in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Plant in a sunny, sheltered position and firm the soil around the roots. Prune to an open vase each winter and thin the fruit for size. The richly colored, red-skinned yellow freestone ripens in late summer and is a favorite for both fresh eating and canning.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; needs a pollen partner because its flowers are largely self-sterile.

directions: Plant near another peach variety, since this one sets poorly without cross-pollination. Keep the graft above the soil and water deeply to settle the roots. The very large, nearly fuzzless yellow freestone with a deep red blush ripens in late summer and is renowned for flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Set in full sun on a slope where frost drains away from the blossoms. Because the fruit is tender and bruises easily, handle it gently and pick just as it softens. The large, creamy white-fleshed freestone blushes red and ripens in late summer with a sweet, aromatic flavor.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; noted for frost-tolerant blossoms.

directions: Plant in full sun and water in well, keeping the union above grade. This variety blooms with good frost resistance and holds its crop through cool springs. The firm, uniform yellow freestone with an orange-red blush ripens in late summer and resists cracking near the pit.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Plant in full sun and prune to an open center for even ripening. Thin the fruit in spring so each peach can reach full size. The large, juicy yellow freestone with a bright red blush ripens in mid to late summer and is prized as a classic fresh-eating peach.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Set the tree in full sun, spreading the roots and firming the soil around them. Water regularly the first year and mulch to keep the root zone cool. The medium to large yellow freestone with a red blush ripens in mid summer and softens quickly for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; sets fruit reliably even in cool, damp weather.

directions: Plant in full sun with the graft above the soil line and water it in. This dependable variety pollinates well and crops even through cool, cloudy springs. The soft, juicy yellow freestone with a light blush ripens in mid summer and is well suited to home gardens and canning.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the root zone evenly moist during establishment. This late-ripening variety also puts on an attractive spring bloom. The large, richly flavored yellow freestone with a deep red blush ripens in late summer, extending the harvest season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; especially resistant to peach leaf curl once established.

directions: Plant in full sun in a cool, damp-climate garden where leaf curl is a problem. Protect the tree with dormant sprays for its first few years until its natural resistance strengthens. The medium yellow freestone with a red blush ripens in mid to late summer and crops heavily in the Pacific Northwest.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill variety for mild-winter regions.

directions: Plant in full sun where winters are mild, since it needs relatively few chill hours to fruit. Thin the heavy crop in spring so the peaches size up properly. The sweet, white-fleshed, low-acid freestone with a pink blush ripens in mid summer and is nearly free of fuzz.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun and prune to an open center to ripen the flattened fruit evenly. Thin in spring so each peach can fill out on the branch. The flat, doughnut-shaped white freestone is very sweet and low in acid, ripening in mid summer with a sunken center around a small pit.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; grown as much for its double red blossoms as its fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun where the striking spring flowers can be enjoyed up close. Enjoy the showy double red blooms first, then thin the fruit for size. The firm, sweet yellow freestone ripens in mid to late summer on a tree that doubles as an ornamental.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a genetic dwarf well suited to large containers.

directions: Grow this natural dwarf in the ground or a large pot on a sunny patio, using rich, free-draining soil. Its compact form needs little pruning beyond removing dead or crossing wood. Showy pink flowers give way to full-sized yellow freestone peaches that ripen in mid summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a very low-chill variety for mild climates.

directions: Plant in full sun in a warm-winter garden, since it fruits with very few chill hours. Water regularly through the first summer to establish the roots. The sweet yellow freestone with a red blush ripens early in the season, often weeks ahead of standard peaches.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill variety that also tolerates warm summers.

directions: Plant in full sun where winters are mild, and water deeply through establishment. This adaptable variety fruits well in both low-chill and hot inland gardens. The sweet, juicy yellow freestone with a red blush ripens in mid summer and is a reliable home-garden choice.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; needs a second peach nearby for pollination.

directions: Plant near another peach variety because its flowers set poorly on their own. Give it full sun and prune to keep light in the canopy. The distinctive freestone has crimson-streaked flesh and a tart, aromatic flavor, ripening in late summer with excellent resistance to leaf curl.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill variety ripening late in warm regions.

directions: Plant in full sun in a mild-winter climate where few peaches ripen this late. Thin the fruit in spring for size and even ripening. The full-flavored yellow freestone with a red blush ripens in late summer, extending the harvest in warm regions.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; one of the earliest-ripening peaches.

directions: Plant in full sun and thin early so the crop reaches usable size on this early type. Keep the root zone watered through the first summer. The firm, red-skinned yellow clingstone ripens very early, opening the peach season well before mid-summer varieties.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this smooth-skinned peach in full sun with the graft above grade and water it in well. Nectarines are especially prone to brown rot, so thin the fruit for airflow and remove any mummies. The large, firm yellow freestone with a bright red skin ripens in late summer with rich, sweet-tart flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the canopy open to reduce disease on the fuzzless fruit. Thin in spring and protect against brown rot as harvest nears. The large, firm yellow freestone with a glossy red-and-gold skin ripens in late summer and holds well on the tree.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; one of the more cold-hardy, disease-tolerant nectarines.

directions: Plant in full sun in a well-drained site and keep the union above the soil. This variety tolerates cold and shows good resistance to brown rot and bacterial spot. The medium yellow freestone with a dark red skin ripens in mid to late summer with rich, tangy flavor.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy, disease-resistant nectarine for cooler areas.

directions: Plant in full sun with good air movement to keep the smooth fruit healthy. Its cold-hardy buds and disease tolerance make it a strong choice for northern gardens. The firm, freestone yellow flesh under a red-blushed skin ripens in late summer and resists cracking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun and prune to an open center for good light and airflow. Thin the crop in spring and watch for brown rot as the fruit ripens. The large, aromatic yellow freestone with a red-blushed skin ripens in mid to late summer with outstanding flavor.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a self-fruitful, widely adapted nectarine.

directions: Plant a single tree in full sun, since it sets fruit without a pollinator. Thin in spring for size and keep the canopy open against disease. The bright red, freestone nectarine with firm yellow flesh ripens in mid summer and is a dependable garden variety.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill nectarine grown for both flowers and fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun where its showy double pink blossoms can be admired in spring. It fruits with modest chill, making it suited to milder-winter gardens. The dark red, freestone nectarine with rich yellow flesh ripens in mid summer following the ornamental bloom.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill white nectarine.

directions: Plant in full sun in a mild-winter garden, since it needs relatively few chill hours. Thin early and keep the canopy open to reduce disease on the smooth skin. The sweet, juicy white freestone with a red blush ripens early to mid season and is low in acid.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill, self-fruitful nectarine for warm regions.

directions: Plant a single tree in full sun in a mild-winter climate, as it fruits without a pollinator. Thin the crop in spring and water well through establishment. The red-skinned, freestone nectarine with yellow flesh ripens in mid to late summer and tolerates hot inland heat.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill, self-fruitful white nectarine.

directions: Plant in full sun in a mild-winter garden where its low chill requirement is met. It sets fruit on its own and benefits from spring thinning for size. The sweet, white-fleshed freestone with a red-blushed skin ripens in late summer and is a longtime California favorite.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill white nectarine.

directions: Plant in full sun in a mild-winter region, thinning the crop in spring for good size. Keep the canopy open to help the smooth fruit stay disease-free. The very sweet, white-fleshed freestone with a red-blushed skin ripens in early to mid summer with low acidity.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun and prune to an open vase for airflow and light. Thin the fruit in spring and guard against brown rot as it ripens. The large, firm yellow freestone with a bright red skin ripens in mid to late summer with sweet, full flavor.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a genetic dwarf good for pots and small gardens.

directions: Grow this natural dwarf in a large container or small bed in full sun with free-draining soil. It stays compact and needs only light pruning, though pairing it with another dwarf improves fruit set. The dark red, freestone nectarine with yellow flesh ripens in mid summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy, self-fruitful nectarine for cooler regions.

directions: Plant a single tree in full sun, as it fruits without a pollinator and tolerates cold well. Keep the canopy open and thin the crop in spring for size. The freestone nectarine with yellow flesh and a red-blushed skin ripens in mid summer and resists brown rot and bacterial spot.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun with the graft above the soil and stake the young tree its first season. This self-fruitful variety ripens ahead of standard Elberta and crops heavily, so thin in spring. The large, juicy yellow freestone with a red blush ripens in mid summer for fresh use and canning.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun and water in well, keeping the union clear of the soil. Thin the fruit in spring so the peaches size up on the branch. The firm, white-fleshed freestone with a red-blushed skin is sweet and low in acid, ripening in mid summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun and prune to an open center to keep the fruiting wood productive. Thin in spring so each peach can reach full size. The firm, sweet yellow freestone with a red blush ripens in early to mid summer and is a productive, dependable home variety.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill early peach for mild regions.

directions: Plant in full sun where winters are mild, since it fruits with modest chill. Thin the crop early so the peaches size up despite the early ripening. The sweet, aromatic yellow-fleshed peach with a red blush ripens early in the season and is nearly free of fuzz.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a very low-chill peach for hot, mild-winter climates.

directions: Plant in full sun in a desert or warm-winter garden where few peaches will fruit. Water regularly in the heat and thin the crop for size. The yellow-fleshed peach with a red blush ripens very early and is one of the first to harvest in low-chill regions.

planting.note: Set bare-root trees in early spring while still dormant.

directions: Plant a grafted tree with the graft union kept a few inches above the finished soil line and water deeply through the first summer. This variety is largely self-fruitful, so a single tree will crop, though a second European plum nearby improves the set. Pick the dark blue, freestone fruit in late summer when it softens slightly and the flesh turns amber and sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in well-drained ground.

directions: Dig a hole wide enough to spread the roots and firm the soil without burying the graft union. This self-fertile prune needs little cross-pollination but rewards summer pruning that opens the canopy to light. Harvest the deep purple, egg-shaped fruit in early autumn once it parts easily from the spur and the sugar-rich flesh pulls cleanly from the pit.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring; shelter from cold spring winds aids fruit set.

directions: Set the tree in fertile, free-draining soil and mulch the root zone to hold moisture. A pollinating partner nearby is helpful, as this old dessert gage sets more reliably with company. Gather the small, round, green-gold fruit in late summer when it turns slightly translucent and honey-sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant with the union above soil and stake young trees against wind rock. This reliably self-fertile variety often overcrops, so thin heavy clusters in early summer to prevent limb breakage. Harvest the rosy-red, oval dessert plums from late summer as they soften and the yellow flesh grows juicy.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; tolerant of exposed, cool sites.

directions: Set this hardy, adaptable tree in most soils and give it little fuss once established. It is self-fertile and needs no partner, making it a dependable choice for a windier garden corner. Pick the small, tart, blue-black fruit in early autumn for cooking, preserves, and country wines.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery trees in early spring.

directions: Set the tree in deep, well-drained loam and keep grass back from the trunk. Plant a compatible European plum nearby, as this large-fruited prune benefits from cross-pollination. Pick the big, blue-black oval fruit in autumn once it yields to gentle pressure and the flesh turns golden and firm.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a pollination partner nearby.

directions: Plant in a warm, sheltered site and firm the roots well. This late variety is not self-fertile, so grow it alongside another European plum for a good crop. Harvest the large, blue-purple fruit in mid-autumn when the bloom is heavy and the flesh softens for cooking or dessert.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in fertile ground.

directions: Set the grafted tree with its union above soil level and mulch to conserve moisture. Provide a nearby European plum for cross-pollination to fill out the crop. Pick the large, reddish-purple oval plums in early autumn once they color fully and the sweet yellow flesh softens.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil and train to an open center for good light. Grow a second European plum nearby to aid fruit set on this large-fruited dessert type. Harvest the very large reddish-purple plums in late summer when the amber flesh grows tender and richly sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; notably cold-hardy.

directions: Set this hardy tree in well-drained soil and protect from harsh winter wind while young. It is self-fertile, cropping alone, and suits colder gardens where other European plums struggle. Pick the round, blue freestone fruit in late summer when the bloom dusts the skin and the flesh sweetens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained ground and stake compact young trees. This self-fertile early plum needs no partner and stays a manageable size for smaller gardens. Harvest the reddish-purple, golden-fleshed fruit in mid to late summer when it softens and turns sweet and aromatic.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; frost-hardy blossom.

directions: Set the tree in most reasonable soils and firm well after planting. Reliably self-fertile with frost-resistant blossom, it crops even in cooler seasons. Pick the dark blue-black cooking plums in late summer as they soften; the greenish-yellow flesh is best stewed or baked.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: Plant in fertile soil against a warm wall or in a sheltered corner to ripen the sugars. A partly self-fertile gage, it fruits better with another European plum close by. Harvest the small, green-yellow, richly flavored gages in late summer when they turn soft and translucent.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a pollination partner.

directions: Set the tree in deep, fertile soil and keep it evenly watered while fruit swells. Not self-fertile, it needs a compatible European plum nearby to crop well. Pick the golden, red-blushed gage-type fruit in late summer when the honeyed flesh softens and parts from the stone.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm position.

directions: Plant in fertile, free-draining soil in the warmest spot you can offer. This self-fertile gage crops on its own and ripens late, extending the gage season. Harvest the round, yellow-green dessert plums in early autumn once they soften and their juice runs sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring against a sunny wall for best ripening.

directions: Train this tree against a warm, sheltered wall, as it needs heat to ripen fully. It is not self-fertile, so grow a compatible European plum nearby. Pick the large, apricot-yellow, red-freckled fruit in mid-autumn when it hangs soft and sugary on the branch.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; hardy and reliable.

directions: Set this vigorous, hardy tree in most soils and give little special care once rooted. It is self-fertile and dependable, a classic culinary plum for cooler gardens. Harvest the oval, pale-yellow fruit in late summer while still firm for cooking and preserving.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in fertile soil.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil and firm the roots after setting. Self-fertile and vigorous, it makes a heavy-cropping culinary tree. Pick the large, red-purple oval plums in late summer while firm for cooking, when the skin fully colors.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; one of the earliest to ripen.

directions: Set the tree in a sheltered, sunny spot and thin the crop if branches become overloaded. It is self-fertile and among the first plums to ripen each season. Harvest the small, yellow-red fruit in midsummer as soon as it softens and sweetens on the tree.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; very late ripening.

directions: Plant this vigorous, hardy tree in well-drained ground and allow room to spread. Reliably self-fertile, it closes the plum season with one of the latest crops. Pick the large, blue-black dual-purpose fruit in early to mid-autumn when the bloom is heavy and the flesh yields.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; compact and reliable.

directions: Set this compact tree in ordinary well-drained soil and keep the young trunk staked. Self-fertile and dependable, it suits smaller gardens and crops heavily. Harvest the small, blue, golden-fleshed plums in late summer for eating fresh or cooking as they soften.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; weeping habit.

directions: Plant this graceful, weeping-branched tree where its arching form has room to show. Self-fertile and productive, it gives good crops without a partner. Harvest the golden-yellow, dual-purpose plums in late summer as they soften for dessert or cooking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm spot.

directions: Set the tree in fertile, well-drained soil and give it a sunny, sheltered aspect. This self-fertile gage crops dependably and doubles for dessert or the kitchen. Pick the large, round, golden-yellow fruit in midsummer when it softens and grows sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a pollinator nearby.

directions: Plant in a warm, sheltered position in fertile soil for the finest flavor. It is not self-fertile, so pair it with a compatible European plum for a full crop. Harvest the round, deep-purple dessert plums in early autumn when the heavy bloom and soft flesh signal ripeness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: Set the tree in fertile, free-draining soil in a warm corner to develop full sweetness. Grow a partner European plum nearby, as it sets more heavily with cross-pollination. Pick the round, red-purple gage fruit in early autumn when the golden flesh softens and turns richly flavored.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator nearby.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and grow a second Japanese plum nearby for pollination. This heavy bearer often needs its fruit thinned to size up the crop. Harvest the round, yellow, translucent fruit in midsummer when the juicy flesh softens and turns sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; spreading, low habit.

directions: Set this low, spreading Japanese plum in full sun and give it room to fill out. Grow a compatible Japanese plum nearby for cross-pollination and thin the heavy set. Harvest the red, amber-fleshed fruit in mid to late summer when it softens and sweetens on the branch.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and pair with another Japanese plum for good fruit set. This is a favored canning and jam plum thanks to its deep red flesh. Pick the dark red, meaty fruit in late summer when it softens and the solid red interior sweetens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator.

directions: Set this large-fruited Japanese plum in full sun and rich, well-drained soil. It needs a compatible Japanese plum nearby, such as Santa Rosa, to pollinate well. Harvest the huge, heart-shaped fruit with dark red flesh in late summer when it softens and the juice turns richly sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and grow a second Japanese plum for cross-pollination. This vigorous tree yields very large fruit that benefits from early thinning. Harvest the big, red-blushed yellow plums in mid to late summer when the sweet flesh softens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and grow a second Japanese plum nearby for pollination. This early bearer sets heavily, so thin the young fruit to improve size. Harvest the round, golden-yellow plums in early summer when the sweet flesh softens.
